root_cause: A bad deploy. The `web` service was released with RELEASE=v2, a build containing a regression that fails its readiness check and returns HTTP 500 on the /orders checkout endpoint. The container process stayed up (so `docker ps` looked healthy) and the database was fine, but the application layer was broken: /healthz returned 503 ('unhealthy: readiness check failing') and /orders returned 500 ('internal error processing order'). The web container's own startup log stated it plainly: 'ERROR: RELEASE=v2 failing readiness and returning 500s on /orders (regression introduced in this release; previous good release was v1)'. The load generator hitting /orders made the 500s continuously visible to users. This was a code/release regression, not an infrastructure, resource, or dependency failure.
actions_taken: 1) Triaged: `docker ps` showed all containers Up, so the fault was application-level, not a crash. 2) Read `docker logs sreft-web`, which named the regression and identified v1 as the last good release. 3) Reproduced the failure from the published port: /healthz -> 503, /orders -> 500. 4) Confirmed the rollback target was safe by checking the unrelated `payments` neighbor, which runs RELEASE=v1 and starts cleanly with no error line. 5) Performed the least-risky remediation: edited only the `web` service's environment in docker-compose.yaml from RELEASE=v2 to RELEASE=v1 (rollback) and recreated just that one service with `docker compose up -d web`. 6) Verified recovery: /healthz -> 200 ('ok'), /orders -> 200 ({"ok":true,"release":"v1"}) across repeated probes, and the startup ERROR line is gone. Confirmed the `payments` and `load` neighbors were never touched and remain healthy. No destructive actions were taken; the change is a single, reversible config rollback.
postmortem: Blameless summary: A routine deploy shipped release v2 of the web service containing a regression in the /orders (checkout) path. The regression caused the readiness probe to fail and every checkout to return HTTP 500, so users saw errors even though the container and database were healthy. Because the container process kept running, coarse 'is it up?' checks stayed green and the bad release remained live serving traffic. Detection: health checks were red and users reported checkout errors; the service's own logs clearly announced the regression and the last good release, which made diagnosis fast. Resolution: rolled the web service back to the previous good release (v1), restoring healthy responses within seconds; no data was affected. Impact: elevated checkout error rate for the window that v2 was live under load. What went well: the release logged an explicit, actionable error naming the regression and the prior good version; rollback was a clean, low-risk config change scoped to a single service. What to improve: (1) gate deploys on the readiness/health check so a release that never becomes healthy is automatically held or rolled back before taking traffic; (2) add a /orders (checkout) smoke test to the deploy pipeline and to post-deploy verification; (3) alert on application-level health (HTTP 503/500 rate) rather than only on container liveness, since a running-but-broken container is invisible to 'is it up?' monitoring; (4) consider progressive/canary rollout so a regression is caught on a small slice of traffic before full exposure. No blame attaches to any individual — the gap was in automated release safety guards, which are the follow-up action items.
